an order of operations.
Hello, hello.
Thanks for taking the time.
I'm Matthew Panzarino.
the Chief Content Officer here at Growthx.
So a lot of work in.

And the content quality, client, deliverable quality realm, kind of the feedback loops that help make the product better and deliver higher quality, kind of involved in the loop between what is actually being produced by the tooling and what we'd love to be produced by the tooling and work closely with the managing editors and content managers across the org as they operate that machinery to help, you know, make it better.
So, pleased to meet you.
And is it Genevieve or Genevieve?
How do you prefer?
I usually use Genevieve when I'm speaking English.
Okay.
Yeah, it makes sense.
Yeah, sounds good.
But yes, pleased to meet you.
Happy to have the time to chat.
I'd love to talk about a few things, kind of just starting off with like, I'm happy to get a little intro from you, by the way.
I have looked at your LinkedIn, so you don't have to give like the full history or anything, but happy to hear about where you're at, what you're doing, like kind of what drew you to the role.
Yeah, absolutely.
So as you could probably see, I've been writing in the B2B space for close to five years now.
But I have been a professional writer for my entire career.
So it's, you know, it's been a really interesting journey.
I've really liked moving into B2B in tech because it just has given me the opportunity to work on like very complex products, which I find really interesting.
Like I really like the challenge of like breaking down these sometimes really complicated technical ideas and making them easy to understand.
And I really love how when you're in content in this space, it's just kind of like, it sounds cheesy, but like every day you're sort of learning something new.
And I'm like, I spend a lot of time like interviewing people about their expertise.
And like, you know, sometimes when you have a really technical ICP, you're like learning a lot about all these different niches and jobs.
So I find that really interesting.
I guess what drew me to kind of, since you know, my general background,
So when Marilia reached out to me, I was like, oh, that's interesting.
And I thought it was cool to hear from her, because as she probably mentioned, I've actually done a fair bit of freelancing work with GrowthX already.
Yeah.
Yeah.
And what like brought, so when I saw that, you know, I could tell just, I'm really active on LinkedIn, so I could tell you guys were hiring some really great people in the space.
I worked with Kerry Chowski and Mara Leighton at Verbatim Labs.
Nice.
Where I worked for, so I saw that, you know, Mara's great.
I saw that she had moved over here.
So I messaged her saying congratulations and like, you know, asking what the deal was.
And then I ended up working on a few different pieces with you guys.
I wrote us, I worked with Jason Gong on a story for the internal publication you guys have, the field guide to GEO.
And then I ended up, I've done three case studies so far with GrowthX.
So I just thought, like, I was just really curious, honestly, to like, you know, I'm interested in the job.
And also just, I thought this was a cool opportunity to talk to more of the team.
And learn like just a bit more about what you guys are doing in terms of client work.
Because obviously the assignments I've done with you guys are not your client work.
Yeah, sounds good.
Sounds good.
Okay.
So there's a handful of things that I'd love to hear from you, kind of just hear you talk about a little bit in more detail.
And then we'll leave some time and you can ask me, you know, all your burning questions about, you know, the way we're headed and what the work we do and all that stuff on the client side.
So first off, talk to me a little bit about like, how do you personally judge whether a piece of content is of quality?
Like, when do you know, like, hey, this feels like quality, this feels like a deliverable that I would be proud of, or, you know, that I think a client would like.
And then the follow up question to that is, when do you know, or what is your judgment call on when it is good enough?
Because we always have, there's an endpoint to client deliverables, right?
Like they have to get shipped at some point, although we'd all love to, you know.
You always think something could be better, but you have to kind of make a call at some point.
So I'd to hear about your kind of rubric for judging content quality overall.
And then, of course, when do you know or how do you find the balance between good enough and inquiry?
It's good to hear you acknowledge that because I think, you know, people talk a lot about tradeoffs around like engineering.
Like I write a lot for software engineers and people are really comfortable talking about like speed, quality tradeoffs with that kind of work.
But like they totally exist with this kind of execution work.
So, you know, it is something to be aware of.
But so I think, I mean, the more context I have for a piece of content, the easier it is for me to judge when it's finished.
And if it's of good quality, because I really need to know who it is for.
And the more I know about who it's for, the better judge I can be in terms of like, who is the person reading it in B2B?
Normally, someone's reading it in a professional capacity.
So, like, what are they trying to get out of this piece of content?
So,
What them do their job better?
What are the stakes?

And then there's the value you want to give to them, the reader, and then there's also like what action are we hoping they will eventually take after they read this piece that benefits the company, the person producing it?
So both those questions have to be answered.

Like I don't think there's really any compromising there because like if you're writing a piece of content that doesn't answer someone's question, it doesn't solve a problem, it doesn't give them useful information, it's really just better not to publish it.

And likewise, if it's not going to be moving the person along, at least in theory, in their buyer journey, because not every prospect is going to move along in the buyer journey, it's not really do, it's not moving the needle for you as a company.
And then so in that, those are kind of like the two goals that need to be met.
And then I think it needs, I mean, just, and then it has to communicate that information in like a concise.
Easy to understand way, which sounds easy, but it's not.
So I just look for content that, you know, I'm like, could anyone pretty much read this?
And hopefully we would like clearly and concisely answer their question and like equip them with whatever they need to meet the needs that they're coming to this piece of content with.
Yeah, great.
Yeah, I mean, that's pretty general, but.

Yeah, yeah, I mean, you know, there's, it's hard, like if I was giving you a specific editorial problem, I'm sure you can speak to it more, more critically, but that is a good framework to understand, you know, kind of where you're at.
And like, I think that relates to the second kind of major point that I'd love to hear from you on, which is like editorial process is a good chunk of what we do here, right?
It is the editor, the act of writing is not so much a part of this role, because the writing is done by the pipelines.
We view them as our writers.
They.
They.
Certainly have their strengths and weaknesses like any writer.
And this is your AI tooling, right?
Correct.
Yes.
Internally, we call the tool Atlas, right?
And we can talk a little bit more about that.
But the pipelines that we use to generate the content or architect the output, because it's not always pure content, right?
It can be an assembly of different objects.
We view those as our writers or content creators.
And the ME's job is really to, like, utilize that pipeline and talk, for lack of a better term, to the pipeline about its process and to understand, you know, okay, what inputs do you need?
Do I need to refine my instructions to you?
So I'd love to hear about, like, an editorial process or content process.
And it may not have been, you know, exactly the one we use.
That's not important to me.
It's more about, I want to hear about a process that you saw a need for improvement in and then executed an improvement on.
And I just wanted to kind of talk through that a little bit.
An editorial process?
Correct.
Yes.
Like if you're working with content, you're working with an editorial process, kind of talk me through like an improvement cycle you did or a need for a new framework or that kind of thing.
I mean, typically when I'm working as a writer, I see editing as like a very collaborative process.
So often, usually when, you know, I've been working as a writer and I'll send over a finished draft, I'm already flagging and highlighting areas on the draft where I could use the editor's input.
And I'm like, I could really use a second opinion here.
I could really use, you know, what do you think of this sentence structure, that paragraph structure.

So I will kind of like preemptively, like call in the editor's help, depending on the type of piece, like sometimes it's more efficient to start from an outline and then work with the editor on that before you move to the draft.
That can save people a lot of time.
But in general, I think, you know, obviously.
Factual accuracy, and then like editing the piece for structure and clarity and flow, like obviously content, everything needs to be accurate.
I'm not sure how fact-checking works with your internal tools.
And then I would always go through editing the structure for logical flow and then editing the actual sentence structure and grammar check would be the last thing I do.
Does that answer your question?
Yeah, sort of.
I mean, I think those are all good.
Those are finances, right?
And hopefully you will be doing that.
And that's great.
I'm kind of looking more towards an articulation of like, okay, you work and practical examples are best, right?
So you're working at Atlassian.
I interviewed Mike a few years ago, by the way.
He's a very interesting dude.
But fascinating company, large, lot of moving parts.
You're working inside a team.
And inevitably, there's going to be some sort of process that you look at and you go, you know what?
What.
This process probably could be better, right?
Have you come across anything like that either there or in previous roles where you've basically been a voice of the room saying, hey, I think we need to improve this process in X, or Z ways?
Yeah, I mean, it might not, my suggestions may not always land, but I can give you two examples that I'm not sure will totally answer your question.
But one would be at Atlassian where we have like multiple layers of internal review and thinking about case studies.
And normally, so the editorial team is going to look at it ourselves, the content team.
Then it goes to the product team, product marketing, because they're going to look at the product information for like, you know, product accuracy, wording, stuff like that.
And then finally, it'll go to the customer.
So something I try to do is highlight and call out specific areas for product marketing to look at.
Because otherwise, often I find they will come in and they'll give like editorial wording and language guidance all over the piece, which.

Kind of moves everything back past the initial editorial stage when like their input is most helpful, when they're really just giving feedback on like the accuracy of how we're talking about the product and whether that aligns with marketing rather than just acting like a second editor when we already had a content editor go over the whole piece.
That can honestly really save a lot of time and honestly just save some sort of politics in a way.
Another example I would give is I worked at Verbatim.
I joined, they were pretty early.
There was only three or four, maybe five other employees when I started.
And the process at that time was to write the piece, write our asset, write one or two supporting LinkedIn posts.
So a change that I suggested and was implemented was to hold off on writing any of that supporting copy until there was a final signed off version of the whole piece.
That had.

It's signed off on by our internal team and the customer, because otherwise you just end up having to reflect all these changes that were made in the body of the piece back into the supporting copy when it would be much faster to just write that up, you know, hewing as closely as possible to the approved copy once you have that done.
So that did save actually a lot of headaches for everyone.
Yeah.
Okay, good.
Thank you.

Yeah, I mean, that's sort of cascading logic gate work is the stuff that is important to have and important to, like, to front when you're dealing with pipelines as writers, because you need to understand, hey, these are the inputs that you're being given at these particular stages.
Here are the instructions that I can give you to improve those.
And if I do give you instructions, am I introducing conflict later on, right?

So, in this example, the conflict being like, oh, yeah, they did some line editing on a piece that was late in the game, but really it's a fundamental thing that should have been talked about earlier, right?
Or set a...
framework for what precise feedback we need from this step, not, we don't need, you know, punctuation editing or thoughts on semantics.
What we need is factual accuracy, whatever.
Interesting.
that, that kind of thing is important because you need to articulate that well.
And I think if I was to sum up the job in kind of one word, it is articulation.
You know, it's explanation of what the decisions and changes you're making are and what, in order to create frameworks or rule sets that can be applied at SCAPE.
So if you are talking to a writer who's a great writer and you're evaluating them as an editor, let's say, let's say they're like, oh, I'd love to be an editor.
You're like, okay, great.
You know, you, you were a great piece the other day.
This is awesome.
How, how did you do this?
And I don't know.
Right.
Like information came in and then I think, you know, some stuff happened.
And then great copy came out.
I've seen many writers like this.
They're absolutely incredible at turning out clean, logical copy, maybe even, you know, narrative flourishes that I never thought of.
And it's like, oh, wow, you know, this is fantastic.
Hey, how'd you do this?
I don't know, right?
And like, that's a problem for us because the articulation part of it is what improves the product quality and increases leverage for everyone.
Because we're delivering content at pretty high scale that most people would basically spend an enormous amount more money and time on.
And that means that you have to find leverage somewhere.
You cannot just tell somebody, hey, I'm going to overwork you, right?
Like, that's not what we're about, right?
We're not in that game.
And that's not good for people and it's not good for the company, right?
So, instead, we're like, hey, I need you to articulate this process well enough so that the pipeline improves and that your job gets easier every day.
Like, that is really the goal.
And if it gets easier.
Every day, that's how we build leverage.
In order to do that, you have to perform exercises like the one you described, where it's like, hey, this feedback needs to come at this step, and it needs to be presented with this framework.
So that's why I'm asking that kind of question.
And then the kind of third major thing that we're really interested in hearing from is like a very specific pragmatic use of AI and AI tooling, LLM tooling.
So can you talk to me about the processes that you use that have AI tooling integrated?
It can be off-the-shelf tools.
can be stuff that you've built.
Just talk to me about a pragmatic example or practical example of time to an AI tool to improve content.
What I use AI for is for processing large volumes of information.
I specifically use it most often for working with interviews because a lot of my pieces start...
I'll use Thank
I mean, honestly, almost all of them start with an expert interview, but depending on the piece, sometimes that's as long as an hour.
And I work, you know, I listen to the recording, but I tend to work with them as transcripts.
That's always been the most time consuming part of my job.
And I really have been able to speed up that part of my job with AI in a way that I didn't expect.
Like this process came together pretty intuitively.
So the transcription and recording tool that I use actually has AI tools built in that work pretty well.
So I usually just use those.
The tool is called Rev.
So your transcript for like an hour long interview, sometimes that's 30 pages, right?
Like it's a lot of very dense information to work through when it's just in its raw state like that.
So, well, and I guess also the transcript itself is done with AI.
Like when I first started and I was doing like journalism, I guess, at the very beginning of my career, I would transcribe interviews by hand.
It would take.
Like not by hand typing, obviously.
So I guess to start with, the transcription itself was done with AI.

But this tool also has AI summarizing and LLM sort of chat style search built in, which I find so helpful because I'm able to start like, so before I dive into the transcript, I have an AI generated list of like key points, major themes of the conversation.
These can give me ideas for subheadings, for sections.

I still do read through the transcript, but I find that I'm able to do that a lot faster and just read in a more focused way because I already kind of know the major areas of the conversation that I'm looking for.

And I really enjoy it for if I've already written something and then I need to go back and find something that was said in the transcript, I can just chat and search instead of like before I would be like control F through this like whole document looking for this information.
So I can just, you know,
Sort of chat with my transcript and literally ask it questions, which saves me a ton of time.
It helps to inform kind of like my outlines, subheadings.
That saves me a lot of time.
I also like to use it for iterating on short form copy.
Like if I'm really stuck on like, oh, what would be a good subheading or what would be a good title?
I find it really helpful for that.
And if I, sometimes I'll use it, if you want to like sort of refine your sentence structure and say, just like make this paragraph a little more concise, I will use it for that.
Although I can't say I end up, I rarely would end up just using what it gives me back.
I would always continue tweaking it.
Okay, good.
And then just like a toolkit question.
What are the platforms that you work with the most?
Like if you were to pick a handful out, use Rev obviously as a tool.
What other tools?
CheckQPT, Claude, you know, Grammarly, like...
What's your kind of suite of tool bags?
So if I'm working with Atlassian, I would use their AI tool, which is called Rovo.
Atlassian is a big percentage of my work, and I can't put their data into commercial tools like ChatGPT.
It's not allowed.
But if I'm working on like my own personal output, I will usually use ChatGPT.
It's handy for like, for example, the case study structure for the case studies I've written with you guys.
I'll take, they all have a pretty, it's like challenge solution results.
So I have a pretty consistent framework there.
So I can even like, if I'm feeling stuck, I can feed it the notes I've already taken from a raw transcript, give it the finalized outline structure of the case study.
And it'll, and I'll be like, hey, put this into full sentences in this approximate structure.
And it'll really like, give me something totally decent to work from and kind of help me just like, skip that step of the drafting process.
Yeah.
And are you using like a custom G?

But I don't really, to be honest, I don't write those type of case studies for my startup clients at the kind of volume anymore that would necessitate a custom GPT, but I think it would be the perfect application for that.
Yeah, great, great.
Cool.
Yeah, thank you.
I appreciate that.
I'm happy to answer any questions you might have about the role or how we work or anything like that.
I'm sure you have a few.
You have a little more visibility than many candidates because you work with us a little bit.
So happy to fill in the other questions.
So what is the goal of the content that you were creating for clients?
That's the thing I wasn't able to get the clearest answer on in my recruiter screen.
So like what types of content are you producing and what kind of outcomes are they supposed to drive?
I mean, I know a little bit because I have written the three case studies, so I get the sense it's like SEO focused, GEO, but I would love to hear from you.
Yeah, SEO and GEO, we actually don't view as goals.
We view those as fundamentals.
So that's like a baseline.
If it's not good for geo or SEO or some combination thereof, why are you doing it at all?
Because those goals are really not antipodal to most other goals.
For instance, if they want high converting content, like bottom of funnel, conversion-driven niche content, why can that not be SEO or geo-optimized?
There's no reason, right?
So is the outcome you're trying to drive, is it usually like search traffic?
Is it bottom of funnel, thought leadership?
So the outcomes are client-specific.
So in the onboarding or in the sales process, we'll go through a discovery process with the client.
Okay, what is it you actually want to do?
Do you just want more traffic?
Okay, let's talk about a volume play, right?
Do you want top of funnel, domain authority?
Okay, let's build out content clusters and start the strategy off of that.
Or if they want something more conversions-focused, then we need to talk about, like, bottom of funnel niche content.
And build out components of content that supports that.

So it can be the primary goals that we hear a lot, or primary outcomes that we hear a lot of, like, of course, overall domain authority, relevance, and citation growth for LLMs, conversions, of course, qualified leads.
You know, all of those are common outcomes, increase in, obviously, would be common outcomes that we hear discussed a lot.
And then we build the content strategy off of that.
So the primary content that we deliver, our work streams that we deliver, the biggest ones are editorial and then programmatic SEO.
And then we have a smattering of other ones.
But editorial being anything that is basically not programmatic.
So let's say a blog post or an article or a series of articles, even a publication, that's really another word.
But it is really editorial.
So for instance, if we signed up with Atlassian, hey, we went to them, what can we do for you?
And they're like, ah, we already have a lot of blog pages and our website's actually ranking pretty well, but we really want to tackle this new ICP, right?
Let's say, you know, Atlassian is going after the construction manager, you know, realm.
Like we've got this new tooling that we think is going to be great for them.
And this is just a really a black hole.
Like everybody in tech knows us, everybody in finance knows us, but people in construction just have no idea we exist, right?
Cool.
We're going to build like AI for construction workers.com or construction managers.com.
That's a terrible brand name, but you get the idea.
We're going to build a publication for you.
That's off.
It's not on Atlassian's website.
It's a publication from scratch.
And then, you know, let's say presented by Atlassian, right?
Like everything you know about AI and construction presented by Atlassian.
And then we will build out an editorial masthead.
We'll build out a series of body of content.
So,
An editorial strategy, and then we'll start shipping regularly and optimize it, make sure it ranks well, and then it grows, et cetera.
So that's like a third work stream that is related to editorial.
But editorial is all of the other things that you might imagine, like blog posts and, you know, competitive comparison pages, all that stuff.
And then programmatic SEO is different.
And all this content is produced with Atlas?
That's right.

Correct.
Okay, cool.

And it's, I would imagine that it's basically like, okay, managing a team of writers, but also having the ability to, like, pop the hood on the writer's brain and make adjustments and have real impact on, like, that architecture.
So if you are interested in, you know, kind of being part of, like, building the architecture.
of content creation and LLMs, not just editing Google Docs.
This is not what this job is, right?
Now, there's a part of it that is, because you need to do that process in order to understand where the gaps are.
So a day in the life might be, hey, I'm running some new tasks, let's call them, which might be articles or programmatic pages or whatever through Atlas.
And then observing the outputs.
You can observe them at each individual stage.
It's not like a black box input-output, right?
It's a pipeline.
You can kind of see what's happening as you go through.
But then you observe the output.
I know this personally, because I do run a few accounts so that I get a ground-level view.
So run the output.
Take a look at the output.
You have the context of what the client wants, what their goals are, what the outcomes they desire are.
You can go through that content and look at it.
Maybe you need to drop it into a Google Doc and do some editing, think about structure and tone and all that stuff.
If you do that work enough with that output, you can come up with a series of improvements that the pipeline probably needs in order to deliver high quality content.
You know, so you're like, hey, it's consistently doing X.
Let's figure out how to articulate to it that we don't want it to do or do want it to do X.
And that process is part of the job.
It's not just, oh, cool, I edited output that came out of this box.
Where's my next batch that came out of this box?
It's operating the pipeline, improving the pipeline, editing, and then moving on to the next cycle with the goal, as I mentioned earlier, of it being easier tomorrow and not always easier tomorrow.
You know, that's the goal.
Interesting.
I really like how analytical this sounds.
Yes, it is.
And that's why so much of the conversation today has been about process, because it is not, hey, are you a good editor?
You should totally come here and work.
I know a lot of good editors.
A lot, trust me.
And some of them are great editors, but not great at the job of articulation either, right?
Most writers are not good at articulation.
And editors get a layer of that.
Yeah, exactly.
And then editors get a layer of that and all that.
So you have to combine the ability, like I have a hip pocket understanding of what good content feels like and looks like, either texturally, the mouthfeel, I call it, of good content.
Like, okay, instinctually, this is good, this is bad.
Okay, now, why?
Why is it good?
Why is it bad?
Talk to me, talk to the pipeline, talk to engineering, right?
And it's like, you're not alone.
You know, the vast majority of the Slack channels are open.
We're pretty transparent about stuff.
There's a lot of brain sharing.
And you have the ability to change inputs and outputs in the pipeline as it goes through to understand what changes that you make affect the output.
But then you also do have help because we are actively developing this product.
So it's not some, you know, black box tool.
part of it is really interesting to me.

You're like, cool, I pay $10 a month.
guess this is what I get, right?

I really don't think it should be doing or vice versa.
I'm going to file a ticket.
Eng looks at it and they're like, oh, yeah, here, we can shift this improvement for you.

Yeah, so who, like, so as a managing editor, like, who are you collaborating with most?
Kind of a combination.
I mean, obviously, I would say in order of operations, account strategists and directors, because they help set the account strategy.

And they've like had all of the discussions at Nauseam with the client to help them understand what we can deliver and to get input from them about what they want, the outcomes that they want, and then architect that overall strategy.
So you're interacting with them because you're like, cool.
Like, I've been running some things that are really.
It's supposed to achieve this goal.
Let's talk about how it's doing and calibrate and that sort of thing.
And then you are working with the pipeline, of course.
You have to think of it as like a co-worker because you're like, hey, I've given it some inputs.
It's a little brainless in this area or it's really smart in this area.
Let's even the load.
And then the development team, the eng team, and the way you interact with them for the most part is just filing tickets.
So it's like, hey, you're talking to your account strategist or director and you're like, oh, man, I've been running this account.
I'm having these issues.
You know what?
I think we should file a ticket on this.
It seems like a bug, right?
Or it seems like an issue.
Or you could just frankly slack somebody because it's pretty open.
So you're like, hey, is anybody else noticing this?
Oh, yeah, I've been noticing it too.
We had a weird link regression last week where links were pretty much amazing.
We have a pretty decent researcher.
And the links that are provided are generally really good.
Our new pipelines actually barely need fact-checking at all.
Previously ran a lot of fact-checking.
when you say researcher, what exactly do you mean?
It's a workflow in the pipeline.
So think of it as like the pipeline is a series of agents or workers that do individual jobs.
And the researcher is one of those workers.
So every time you run an article, research is done on it.
So think of it very much like giving an assignment to a writer.
Somebody gave you an assignment to write about something you've never heard about before.
The first thing you've got to do is research it.
So that's what it does.
And what typically is the gap?
Well, I'm sure this like varies all the time, but I'm just curious, like how close is the output of your pipeline to being client ready most of the time?
Yeah, good question.
Great question.
It varies.
And in the best case scenario, about 10 to 15 minutes worth of post-processing and then about five minutes worth of human editing.
And it's good to present to the client.
In a worst case scenario, it could be an hour.
scenario, of It I'm

and a half of trying to figure out what's, you know, where it went awry, or just applying very hyper-specific requirements that the client has, because it takes time to engineer the pipelines into a shape where they're delivering very specific outcomes.
We have a client, as an example, who's a women's supplement company that specifically deals with supplementation for perimenopausal, postmenopausal, and premenopausal women.
And so they have an MD reviewer, an OB reviewer, and an ND reviewer.
So naturopathic doctor, OB-GYN reviewer, a lawyer who reviews it, and a co-founder who reviews it.
